_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Walton County, GA. Record survival, courthouse history. Walton County, formed 1818, has records from its formation. No major courthouse fire or war damage.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early courthouses replaced. No major fire or war damage.

record_losses.cause: No major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Walton County records survive from the county's 1818 formation with good continuity. The courthouse at Monroe was not destroyed during the Civil War.
record_losses.narrative_impact: {ancestor_name}'s records in Walton County likely survive — deeds, wills, and court minutes run continuously from 1818.
record_losses.source: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.repository: Walton County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Archives microfilm

wills_estates.repository: Walton County Probate Court; GA Archives

court_minutes.repository: Walton County Clerk of Superior Court

substitute_sources.description: The 1820 lottery distributed Walton County lots.
substitute_sources.repository: GA Department of Archives and History
substitute_sources.source: Cadle, Georgia Land Surveying History and Law (1991).

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Methodist churches in Walton County. Church minute books supplement civil records.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Mercer University Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for marriage and membership documentation.

narrative_hooks: Walton County's courthouse records run from 1818 without major losses — {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court minutes likely survive.
The Georgia Land Lottery drew {ancestor_name}'s lot in Walton County — the state-level lottery records document who won what, regardless of what the county records show.
Georgia vital registration began in 1919 — before that, church records and family Bibles are the primary sources for births, marriages, and deaths in Walton County.
If {ancestor_name} was a sharecropper in Walton County, crop lien filings at the county courthouse document the debt cycle — the legal instruments of a system designed to keep families in place.
source_references: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.
Cadle, Farris W. Georgia Land Surveying History and Law. University of Georgia Press, 1991.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Georgia. Atlanta, 1937-1942.
